items.title: Code building block shape
items.description: A CodeBuildingBlock is a structured brick the platform composes into features. It carries a BlockId, Name, Purpose, ProblemSolved, RequiredInputs, GeneratedFiles, CodePatternSummary, Dependencies, SecurityRules, ValidationRules, TestPattern, PlaywrightPattern, and FailureModes, plus links to the supporting knowledge items, code-graph nodes, experience entries, and generator template, and a confidence level. It captures how to solve a recurring problem, not a frozen copy of one solution.
items.applicability: The schema every concrete block in this pack instantiates, and the contract the generator and reasoning engine read when composing a feature.
items.example: The secure-login block fills this shape: purpose is authenticate a user, required inputs are user store and route, generated files are a controller and views, validation includes anti-forgery, and it links to the auth-hardening knowledge item.
items.limitation: Implementation implication: store blocks as metadata plus a pattern summary so they adapt to context rather than pasting literal code. Test implication: assert every block defines at least one TestPattern and one FailureMode. Generator implication: the generator refuses a block missing RequiredInputs. Honest boundary: the shape standardises description, not correctness; a well-formed block can still be the wrong choice for a given task.

items.title: Secure login block
items.description: Authenticates a user against a stored credential. Required inputs: a user store, a sign-in route, and a redirect target. Generated files: a login controller action, a login view, and the wiring to the user store. Validation rules: anti-forgery on the POST, server-side credential checks, generic error messages that do not reveal which field was wrong.
items.applicability: Any feature that needs an authenticated entry point; composes with the hashing and lockout blocks.
items.example: A request for an admin login emits the action and view, calls the hashing block to verify the password, and the lockout block to gate repeated failures.
items.limitation: Implementation implication: never authenticate on the client and never echo whether the username or password was incorrect. Test implication: xUnit asserts valid credentials sign in and invalid ones do not; Playwright drives both paths. Failure modes: timing leaks, missing anti-forgery, plaintext comparison. Honest boundary: this block authenticates only; authorisation, session expiry, and MFA are separate concerns it does not cover.

items.title: Password hashing block (PBKDF2)
items.description: Hashes and verifies passwords using a salted, iterated PBKDF2 derivation. Required inputs: the plaintext password and, for verification, the stored hash and salt. Generated files: a hashing service and its test. Security rules: per-user random salt, a strong iteration count, constant-time comparison on verify, and never logging the plaintext.
items.applicability: Used by the login block and any credential-setting flow such as registration or password reset.
items.example: On registration the service derives a hash with a fresh salt and stores both; on login it re-derives with the stored salt and compares in constant time.
items.limitation: Implementation implication: store salt and iteration count alongside the hash so verification is reproducible, and raise iterations over time. Test implication: assert the same password with different salts yields different hashes and that verify succeeds only for the right password. Failure modes: reused salt, fast comparison, logging plaintext. Honest boundary: hashing protects stored credentials only; it does not defend the transport channel or weak user-chosen passwords.

items.title: Login lockout block
items.description: Limits brute-force attempts by counting consecutive failures and locking an account for a cool-down window. Required inputs: the user identity, a failure threshold, and a lockout duration. Generated files: lockout state on the user store and the guard logic. Validation rules: count only failed attempts, reset on success, and surface a generic locked message.
items.applicability: Composes with the secure-login block to harden any sign-in path.
items.example: After five failed attempts the account is locked for fifteen minutes; a sixth attempt is refused even with the correct password until the window passes.
items.limitation: Implementation implication: persist the failure count and lock-until timestamp so lockout survives restarts. Test implication: assert the threshold triggers a lock and that a successful login resets the count. Failure modes: counting successes, in-memory-only state, lock bypass on restart. Honest boundary: lockout slows credential stuffing but can enable denial of service against a known username, so pair it with monitoring.

items.title: Audit event block
items.description: Records who did what and when for sensitive actions. Required inputs: the actor, the action, the target, and an outcome. Generated files: an audit entity, a write helper, and its test. Validation rules: audit rows are append-only, carry a UTC timestamp, and are written in the same transaction as the action they describe.
items.applicability: Attached to authentication, approvals, postings, and any state-changing operation that must be traceable.
items.example: Approving an invoice writes an audit row capturing the approver, the invoice id, the approve action, and the timestamp, alongside the approval itself.
items.limitation: Implementation implication: never expose update or delete on audit rows and write them transactionally with the action. Test implication: assert an action produces exactly one audit row and that rows cannot be edited. Failure modes: missing audit on a code path, audit written outside the transaction, mutable rows. Honest boundary: an audit trail records what the application did, not external tampering at the database or OS level.

items.title: Maker/checker workflow block
items.description: Separates the person who prepares a change (maker) from the one who approves it (checker), so no single user can both create and authorise a sensitive action. Required inputs: the document, the maker identity, and the checker identity. Generated files: status fields, the submit and approve actions, and a guard. Validation rules: the checker must differ from the maker, and the action takes effect only after approval.
items.applicability: Required for financial postings, master-data changes, and other high-impact operations.
items.example: A user submits a payment for approval; a different authorised user approves it; only then does the payment post, with both identities recorded.
items.limitation: Implementation implication: enforce maker-not-equal-checker in the domain, not the UI, and tie approval to the audit block. Test implication: assert self-approval is rejected and that an unapproved document does not post. Failure modes: same-user approval, action taking effect before approval, missing audit. Honest boundary: segregation of duty reduces single-actor fraud but cannot stop collusion between two authorised users.

items.title: EF migration-safe change block
items.description: Evolves the schema with an additive, backward-compatible EF Core migration. Required inputs: the entity change and a migration name. Generated files: the migration class, the updated model snapshot, and the entity edit. Validation rules: prefer adding nullable columns or new tables, apply with Migrate on SQL Server, and keep the snapshot consistent.
items.applicability: Used whenever a block needs new persistent state, so schema changes stay predictable.
items.example: Adding a lockout timestamp emits a migration that adds a nullable column and updates the snapshot, applied cleanly on an empty and a populated database.
items.limitation: Implementation implication: never hand-edit the snapshot into inconsistency and never mix EnsureCreated with Migrate on the same real database. Test implication: assert the migration applies on an empty database and is idempotent. Failure modes: lossy column changes, snapshot drift, destructive defaults. Honest boundary: this block covers additive change; genuinely destructive migrations require explicit human approval and a backup outside its scope.

items.title: Generic CRUD block (list/create/edit/deactivate)
items.description: Provides list, create, edit, and deactivate operations for a simple entity. Required inputs: the entity name and its fields. Generated files: a controller, list and edit views, and a service with its test. Validation rules: list queries project to lightweight rows that exclude large text columns, deactivate is a soft flag rather than a hard delete, and server-side validation runs on create and edit.
items.applicability: The default scaffold for master-data and reference entities that need basic management screens.
items.example: A supplier entity emits a paged list that selects only id, name, and status, plus create and edit forms and a deactivate toggle, never a destructive delete.
items.limitation: Implementation implication: never materialise large content columns in the list query and never hard-delete by default. Test implication: assert the list excludes heavy columns and that deactivate sets a flag rather than removing the row. Failure modes: full-entity list loads, missing server validation, hard delete losing history. Honest boundary: this block handles simple entities; complex documents with lifecycles use the document-lifecycle block instead.

items.title: Report endpoint block
items.description: Exposes a read-only report computed from data with filters. Required inputs: the data source, the filter parameters, and the output columns. Generated files: a report controller action, a view model, and a view. Validation rules: queries are read-only, use separate count and page queries rather than group-by-constant aggregates, and read from a cached snapshot for any external-service status.
items.applicability: Any dashboard tile, ledger, or summary screen that aggregates stored data for display.
items.example: A trial-balance report takes a date range, runs bounded per-account queries, and renders totals without ever calling an external service on the request path.
items.limitation: Implementation implication: never block the request path on an external service and avoid group-by-constant projections that can hang SQL Server. Test implication: assert the endpoint returns quickly with a seeded dataset and that filters narrow results correctly. Failure modes: page hangs from clever aggregation, materialising large columns, synchronous health calls. Honest boundary: this block presents data; it does not guarantee the underlying figures are business-correct.

items.title: Playwright proof block
items.description: Generates an end-to-end browser test that drives a user flow against the running app. Required inputs: the flow steps and the expected observable outcome. Generated files: a Playwright spec and any fixture setup. Validation rules: run against a freshly started instance with a known seed, assert on visible outcomes, and ensure the test fails when the flow is broken.
items.applicability: Paired with any user-facing block to prove the feature works as a user experiences it.
items.example: For the login block it opens the page, signs in with valid and invalid credentials, and asserts the dashboard and the error each appear as expected.
items.limitation: Implementation implication: seed deterministically and wait on real signals, not fixed sleeps. Test implication: a negative run (broken flow) must fail the proof, confirming it actually checks behaviour. Failure modes: flaky waits, non-deterministic seed, assertions that always pass. Honest boundary: a proof verifies the scripted path only; it cannot cover every input or edge case.

items.title: xUnit service test block
items.description: Generates focused unit and integration tests for a service. Required inputs: the service under test and its expected behaviours. Generated files: an xUnit test class with arrange-act-assert cases, using an in-memory or SQLite database where persistence is involved. Validation rules: each behaviour and its failure path has a test, and tests are independent and repeatable.
items.applicability: Paired with any service-bearing block to climb the test rung of the readiness ladder.
items.example: For the hashing block it asserts verify succeeds for the right password, fails for the wrong one, and that two hashes of the same password differ by salt.
items.limitation: Implementation implication: use a fresh database per test with EnsureCreated on SQLite, not a shared mutable one. Test implication: assert both the happy path and at least one failure path per behaviour. Failure modes: shared state between tests, only-happy-path coverage, hidden order dependence. Honest boundary: passing unit tests prove the covered behaviours, not the absence of bugs in uncovered paths.

items.title: Production smoke test block
items.description: Generates a fast post-deploy check that the core pages and health endpoints respond. Required inputs: the base URL and the list of critical routes. Generated files: a smoke script that requests each route and asserts a quick successful response. Validation rules: each core page must return promptly, and a hung endpoint (started with no completion) is treated as a failure.
items.applicability: Run immediately after deployment or a fresh-clone start to confirm the app is alive.
items.example: The script requests the home, projects, knowledge, and models pages and asserts each returns a success status well under a second.
items.limitation: Implementation implication: assert on both status and response time so a slow hang is caught, not just an error code. Test implication: a deliberately stalled route must fail the smoke run. Failure modes: checking status only, missing a critical route, tolerating multi-second responses. Honest boundary: a smoke test confirms basic liveness, not functional correctness or behaviour under load.

items.title: Backup/restore script block
items.description: Generates scripts to back up and restore the application database safely. Required inputs: the connection target and a backup location. Generated files: a backup script, a restore script, and a verify step. Validation rules: take a backup before any destructive operation, verify the backup is readable, and make restore explicit and confirmed.
items.applicability: Required before destructive migrations or risky operations, and for routine recovery drills.
items.example: Before a schema change the backup script writes a timestamped dump and verifies it can be read back before the change proceeds.
items.limitation: Implementation implication: verify the backup before relying on it and never auto-run restore without confirmation. Test implication: assert backup then restore reproduces a known row set. Failure modes: unverified backups, silent overwrite on restore, missing pre-destructive backup. Honest boundary: a backup script protects against data loss only if it is exercised; an untested backup is not a guarantee.

items.title: Stock movement block (moving-average)
items.description: Records inventory movements and maintains a moving-average cost. Required inputs: the item, the quantity, the direction (in or out), and the unit cost for inflows. Generated files: a stock movement entity, the valuation service, and tests. Validation rules: recompute the weighted-average cost on each inflow, value outflows at the current average, and never allow negative stock without an explicit policy.
items.applicability: Inventory-bearing modules where stock value must stay consistent with quantity on hand.
items.example: Receiving 100 units at 10 then 100 at 12 sets the average to 11; issuing 50 reduces quantity and values the issue at 11.
items.limitation: Implementation implication: store amounts as fixed-precision decimals and apply movements in a serialised order to avoid races. Test implication: assert the average after mixed receipts matches the weighted calculation and that issues use the running average. Failure modes: floating-point drift, out-of-order application, unguarded negative stock. Honest boundary: moving-average is one valuation method; FIFO, LIFO, and standard costing are different blocks this one does not implement.

items.title: Accounting posting block (double-entry)
items.description: Posts balanced double-entry journal lines through a single posting engine. Required inputs: the posting lines (account, debit, credit) and a posting date. Generated files: the posting service, the GL entry writes, and tests. Validation rules: total debits equal total credits within currency tolerance, at least one debit and one credit, postings target only leaf accounts, and posted entries are immutable.
items.applicability: Every financial document (invoice, payment, journal) reduces to this block to write the general ledger.
items.example: A rent payment posts debit Rent Expense and credit Bank for equal amounts through the engine, which writes two GL rows under one voucher reference.
items.limitation: Implementation implication: route all postings through the one engine so the balance rule cannot be bypassed. Test implication: assert unbalanced, single-line, and zero-total postings are all rejected. Failure modes: bypassing the engine, posting to group accounts, editing posted entries. Honest boundary: the block enforces structural balance, not the business correctness of the chosen accounts.

items.title: Document lifecycle block
items.description: Adds a controlled state machine to a business document: Draft, Submit, Approve or Reject, and after posting only Cancel or Reverse, with posted documents immutable. Required inputs: the document type and its allowed transitions. Generated files: status fields, transition actions, and guards. Validation rules: only declared transitions are permitted, posted rows cannot be edited, and corrections are made by reversal.
items.applicability: Any document whose history must stay auditable, in contrast to simple CRUD entities.
items.example: An approved invoice is locked; correcting it posts a reversal and a new corrected invoice rather than editing the original.
items.limitation: Implementation implication: enforce transitions in one domain method and forbid mutation of posted rows everywhere. Test implication: assert an illegal transition and an edit of a posted document are both rejected. Failure modes: free-text status, editable posted rows, transitions enforced only in the UI. Honest boundary: the lifecycle controls state changes, not whether a given posting is the right business decision.

items.title: Import/export block with rejected-row report
items.description: Imports or exports tabular data with per-row validation and an explicit rejected-row report. Required inputs: the file format, the target entity, and the column mapping. Generated files: the import/export service, the rejected-row report, and tests. Validation rules: validate each row independently, accept valid rows while collecting rejects with reasons, and never abort the whole batch for one bad row.
items.applicability: Bulk data onboarding and extraction where partial success and clear feedback matter.
items.example: Importing 1,000 supplier rows accepts 980 and produces a rejected-row report listing the 20 failures with the specific reason for each.
items.limitation: Implementation implication: process rows in a way that isolates failures and always emit the reject report even on full success (empty). Test implication: assert a mixed file imports valid rows and reports each invalid one with a reason. Failure modes: whole-batch abort on one error, silent row loss, missing reasons. Honest boundary: the block validates structure and declared rules per row, not cross-row business consistency beyond those rules.
